AI.motivation: Prior surgical vision-language models collapse multi-dimensional, hierarchical surgical knowledge (fine-grained actions, mid-level steps, global procedural context) into a single flat embedding space, ignoring cross-level semantic containment and weakening long-range dependency modeling.
AI.method: A hyperbolic surgical video-language pre-training framework that explicitly preserves hierarchical structure by mitigating structural false negatives from procedural context and enforcing semantic consistency between parent phases and their constituent child steps.
AI.result: Consistent gains in zero- and few-shot phase recognition across multiple surgical benchmarks and different procedures/institutions.
AI.conclusion: Preserving the inherent hierarchical structure of surgical knowledge (via hyperbolic embedding and cross-level consistency) is more effective than flat embedding approaches for surgical phase recognition, especially in low-data scenarios.

title: Rethinking the Role of Feature Engineering and Learning Strategies in Few-Shot Hidden Emotion Recognition
summary: In this paper, we present the solution developed by our team, XInsight Lab, which achieved first place in Track 3 of the 4th EI-MIGA-IJCAI Challenge with a test accuracy of 0.76923. To address the challenge of weak and sparse implicit emotion evidence in long videos, this paper extends the winning solution from the previous competition and proposes a compact multi-modal temporal modeling framework. The framework integrates and evaluates the effects of multi-source features, including 2D/3D skeletons, facial expression Blendshapes, DINOv2/v3 vision foundation models, X-CLIP video features, and Gemini semantic priors. Architecturally, we propose a cross-attention mechanism that utilizes static pose features, denoted as Base, as the Query and dynamic micro-motion differential features, denoted as Offset, as the Key and Value. By capturing local relative velocities, this mechanism eliminates static biases related to individual body shape and identity. Concurrently, an adaptive pooling method based on Multiple Instance Learning is employed to extract instantaneous emotions while suppressing background noise in long sequences. Finally, the paper reveals the representation collapse phenomenon of general vision foundation models in micro-dynamic tasks, and analyzes the underlying mechanisms where networks fall into public-leaderboard-driven pseudo-generalization due to shortcut learning and rote memorization.

AI.tldr: XInsight Lab won first place in Track 3 of the 4th EI-MIGA-IJCAI Challenge (0.76923 test accuracy) by extending their previous winning solution with a compact multi-modal temporal modeling framework for weak/sparse implicit emotion evidence in long videos, using cross-attention (static pose as Query, dynamic micro-motion as Key/Value), MIL-based adaptive pooling, and analyzing representation collapse in vision foundation models.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of weak and sparse implicit emotion evidence in long videos, particularly the difficulty of detecting subtle emotional cues that are easily overwhelmed by background noise and static body shape biases.
AI.method: A compact multi-modal temporal modeling framework integrating 2D/3D skeletons, facial Blendshapes, DINOv2/v3, X-CLIP, and Gemini semantic priors; cross-attention mechanism using static pose (Base) as Query and dynamic micro-motion differential (Offset) as Key/Value to eliminate static biases; adaptive MIL-based pooling for extracting instantaneous emotions while suppressing background noise.
AI.result: Achieved first place in Track 3 with 0.76923 test accuracy; revealed representation collapse in general vision foundation models for micro-dynamic tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cross-attention and MIL-based pooling effectively handle weak implicit emotions in long videos; however, general vision foundation models suffer from representation collapse and public-leaderboard-driven pseudo-generalization due to shortcut learning and rote memorization.

title: Decodable Is Not Grounded: A Vision-Ablation Arbiter for VLM Spatial Reasoning
summary: The standard way to read latent knowledge out of a model, a linear probe confirmed by a steering recovery, can systematically overstate what a vision-language model (VLM) actually grounds in the image. We show this on spatial reasoning, where the error is invisible to both probing and steering yet exposed by a one-line causal control: replacing the image with a gray blank. Probes decode the within-axis answer at 73--97% across axes, and a training-free projection lifts a near-chance axis from 59% to 79%, exactly the signature of unlocking latent knowledge. The blank-image arbiter refutes it, revealing three grounding regimes that probing conflates: an axis can be grounded (vision-dependent, correct), a prior (vision-independent, with its decode and its apparent recovery a directional default rather than perception), or, surprisingly, inverted: decodable, causally controllable, but deployed with the wrong sign, so the model scores below chance and the error requires looking. The taxonomy holds across the studied VLMs: in fourteen models spanning six language-model families and 2B--27B, horizontal is grounded, vertical is a prior, and depth is inverted, with the inversion emerging at scale within families. The decode-versus-deploy inversion replicates on seven of eight models across five families, and the minimal edit that re-deploys it varies with geometry: a training-free rotation matches a trained edit on the cleanest model, while distributed inversions need a trained low-rank edit, tracing a per-model correction-complexity spectrum. The cheap, self-calibrating arbiter cleanly separates grounded perception, inverted perception, and prior substitution; we argue it should be a default control for latent-knowledge and steering claims in VLMs.

AI.tldr: Linear probing and steering methods can overstate what VLMs actually perceive in images. A simple blank-image control reveals three grounding regimes: grounded (true vision-dependent), prior (vision-independent default), and inverted (decodable but deployed with wrong sign). Across 14 VLMs, horizontal is grounded, vertical is a prior, depth is inverted—an error that emerges at scale and requires different correction strategies.
AI.motivation: Standard methods (linear probing + steering recovery) systematically overstate what VLMs actually ground in images, especially on spatial reasoning. The error is invisible to both probing and steering, motivating a simple causal control (blank image) to expose it.
AI.method: Use a blank-image arbiter (replacing image with gray blank) as a causal control to separate vision-dependent from vision-independent decoding. Validate across 14 VLMs (2B–27B, six LM families) on spatial reasoning axes (horizontal, vertical, depth). Apply training-free projection and trained low-rank edits to test controllability.
AI.result: Three grounding regimes identified: grounded (vision-dependent, correct), prior (vision-independent, directional default), inverted (decodable, controllable, but wrong sign). Horizontal is grounded, vertical is a prior, depth is inverted across all models. Inversion emerges at scale. Different models require different correction complexity (rotation vs. low-rank edit).
AI.conclusion: The blank-image arbiter should be a default control for latent-knowledge and steering claims in VLMs, as it cleanly separates grounded perception, inverted perception, and prior substitution that standard methods conflate.

title: WarpHammer: Densifying Scene Warps with 3D Object Priors for Extreme View Synthesis
summary: Projection-conditioned novel view synthesis (NVS) warps an explicit 3D reconstruction of the input view into the target camera and conditions a generator on the warped rendering. This works well for small viewpoint changes but degrades sharply under large orbital motion: the warp becomes sparse around the orbited object, where hidden surfaces dominate the new view and mirror-like artifacts emerge, causing the generator to lose both pixel content and the implicit camera cue carried by the warp. We introduce WarpHammer, a training-free framework that resolves this failure mode by augmenting the warped scene with an explicit 3D reconstruction of the object obtained from a native 3D generative prior (e.g., SAM3D). The reconstructed object adds missing foreground surfaces and occludes background points that should no longer be visible, restoring both appearance and camera cues without fine-tuning the base model. The same explicit object representation further unlocks a capability current NVS pipelines do not support: incorporating auxiliary views of the object from sources outside the target scene, for example, a casual snapshot of a car paired with a manufacturer studio shot of the same model. We process the reference and auxiliary images jointly with a pretrained multi-view geometry foundation model, which predicts a unified point cloud that we fuse into the 3D object reconstruction. This yields substantially more faithful geometry than single-image reconstruction, without requiring user-provided camera poses for the auxiliary views. On five benchmarks, WarpHammer produces stable novel views at viewpoint deviations where strong baselines collapse, and is the first scene-level NVS method that can naturally fuse auxiliary, pose-unknown object views from an external source.

AI.tldr: WarpHammer is a training-free framework that improves projection-conditioned novel view synthesis under large viewpoint changes by augmenting warped scenes with explicit 3D object reconstructions from generative priors, and further enables fusion of auxiliary object views from external sources without requiring camera poses.
AI.motivation: Existing projection-conditioned NVS methods work well for small viewpoint changes but fail under large orbital motion due to sparse warping, hidden surfaces, and mirror-like artifacts that degrade both pixel content and camera cues.
AI.method: WarpHammer augments the warped scene with explicit 3D reconstruction from a generative prior (e.g., SAM3D), adds missing foreground surfaces, occludes invisible background points, and uses a multi-view geometry foundation model to fuse external auxiliary views into the 3D reconstruction without requiring camera poses.
AI.result: On five benchmarks, WarpHammer produces stable novel views at large viewpoint deviations where strong baselines collapse, and is the first scene-level NVS method to naturally fuse auxiliary pose-unknown object views.
AI.conclusion: Explicit 3D object reconstruction can effectively resolve the large-motion failure mode in NVS and unlock cross-source view fusion, all without fine-tuning or user-provided camera poses.

title: Learning from Failure: Inference-Time Self-Improvement for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents, which leverage multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to operate computers and complete tasks, have attracted significant attention for their utility and versatility. A major challenge in developing these agents is collecting large-scale, high-quality trajectories. The standard approach generates synthetic data through a self-improving loop: an agent is placed in a verifiable environment and iteratively fine-tuned on its successful trajectories. Despite its effectiveness, this paradigm exploits only successful trajectories and discards the failed ones, even though failures carry rich information about a model's weaknesses. In this work, we explore a complementary failure-driven self-improvement loop, a data-centric paradigm that turns failed trajectories into agent improvements. Specifically, we employ an LLM to diagnose failure modes, propose inference-time solutions, and generate code patches -- lightly verified by humans -- that upgrade the agent. We validate this approach with the state-of-the-art OpenCUA-72B model on the OSWorld benchmark, improving the success rate from 42.3% to 48.9%, a gain of 6.6 percentage points, without any additional training cost and with only modest inference overhead. Our results demonstrate that failure-driven self-improvement is a viable complement to success-based pipelines, enabling more efficient agent improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a failure-driven self-improvement loop for computer-use agents, where LLMs diagnose failures from unsuccessful trajectories and generate code patches (lightly verified by humans) to upgrade the agent. Applied to OpenCUA-72B on OSWorld, it improves success rate from 42.3% to 48.9% (+6.6%) without additional training cost.
AI.motivation: Current self-improving loops for computer-use agents only exploit successful trajectories and discard failed ones, even though failures contain rich information about a model's weaknesses. The authors aim to turn failed trajectories into agent improvements.
AI.method: A failure-driven self-improvement loop where: (1) an LLM diagnoses failure modes from failed trajectories, (2) proposes inference-time solutions, and (3) generates code patches that are lightly verified by humans to upgrade the agent.
AI.result: Applied to OpenCUA-72B on OSWorld benchmark, the method improves success rate from 42.3% to 48.9% (+6.6 percentage points) without additional training cost and with only modest inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Failure-driven self-improvement is a viable and complementary approach to success-based pipelines, enabling more efficient agent improvement by leveraging otherwise discarded failure information.

title: CLIMB: Centroid-Based Hierarchical Memory for Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning (OCSSL) aims to learn representations from a continuous stream of unlabeled data, without knowledge of task boundaries and under memory constraints. Existing methods rely either on replay buffers that exploit latent space structure, or on regularization alone. We present CLIMB (Continual Learning with Intelligent Memory Bank), which combines both simultaneously. Our method introduces a hierarchical centroid-based memory, bounded in total number of stored images, combined with knowledge distillation on replayed examples to limit representation drift. The memory groups similar images into centroids, providing hard-to-discriminate examples for contrastive learning while covering the diversity of observed distributions. Experiments on Split CIFAR-100 and Split ImageNet-100, on standard benchmarks from the state-of-the-art as well as a new protocol with irregular task distributions show that CLIMB outperforms state-of-the-art OCSSL methods.

AI.tldr: CLIMB combines a hierarchical centroid-based replay buffer with knowledge distillation to achieve state-of-the-art performance in online continual self-supervised learning.
AI.motivation: Existing OCSSL methods rely either on replay buffers or regularization alone; the paper aims to combine both simultaneously for better representation learning under memory constraints and without task boundaries.
AI.method: CLIMB uses a hierarchical centroid-based memory that groups similar images into centroids (bounded in total stored images) and applies knowledge distillation on replayed examples to limit representation drift, providing hard-to-discriminate examples for contrastive learning.
AI.result: CLIMB outperforms state-of-the-art OCSSL methods on Split CIFAR-100 and Split ImageNet-100 under standard benchmarks and a new protocol with irregular task distributions.
AI.conclusion: Combining replay buffers with regularization via hierarchical memory and knowledge distillation is an effective strategy for online continual self-supervised learning.

title: Editing Everything Everywhere All at Once
summary: Editing multiple elements of an image in a single forward pass is a practical alternative to multi-turn image manipulation, offering improved efficiency and potentially better harmonization. However, when several instructions target different regions, semantic interference often leads to attribute leakage and poor edit disentanglement, especially as the number of edits increases. In this work, we propose MICE (Multi-Instance Concurrent Editing), a training-free strategy for scalable multi-instance image editing with Multimodal Diffusion Transformers. MICE modifies the additive bias of joint attention to regulate interactions between instance-specific edit instructions, latent, and context tokens identified via user-provided segmentation masks. Specifically, MICE allows intra-instance attention, penalizes interactions between neighboring region tokens, and suppresses unrelated cross-instance attention. As a result, our method enforces attribute binding while preserving global visual consistency. We evaluate MICE on LoMOE-Bench and introduce MICE-Bench, a more challenging benchmark with an average of 8.5 concurrent edits per image. The experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms strong baselines and recent competitors in terms of visual quality preservation and faithfulness to the editing instructions.

AI.tldr: MICE is a training-free method for multi-instance image editing that modifies joint attention biases in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers to prevent semantic interference, enabling concurrent edits on multiple image regions in a single forward pass.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-instance image editing suffers from semantic interference (attribute leakage) when multiple instructions target different regions, worsening with more edits. A single-pass solution is needed for efficiency and better harmonization.
AI.method: MICE modifies the additive bias of joint attention in Multimodal Diffusion Transformers to: (1) allow intra-instance attention, (2) penalize interactions between neighboring region tokens, and (3) suppress unrelated cross-instance attention, guided by user-provided segmentation masks.
AI.result: MICE outperforms strong baselines and recent competitors on LoMOE-Bench and the newly introduced MICE-Bench (avg. 8.5 concurrent edits per image) in visual quality preservation and faithfulness to editing instructions.
AI.conclusion: A training-free attention regulation strategy can effectively disentangle multiple concurrent edits in diffusion transformers, enforcing attribute binding while maintaining global visual consistency.

title: Deep Spectral Models for Robust Dental Shape Generation
comment: Accepted for publication at the Journal of Machine Learning for Biomedical Imaging (MELBA) https://melba-journal.org/2026:016
summary: Accurate modeling of dental crown morphology is fundamental for diagnosis, orthodontic planning, and computer-aided restoration design. However, datasets suitable for training such models are typically limited in size. We present ToothForge, a deep spectral generative framework that models dental crown geometries from compact, intrinsic representations. By operating in the spectral domain, ToothForge learns a latent manifold of 3D tooth shapes through synchronized spectral embeddings, ensuring consistent modeling across samples with varying connectivity. Spectral synchronization mitigates the instability of Laplace-Beltrami eigenbases and enables efficient learning in a low-dimensional space. The framework is thoroughly evaluated through robustness analysis, ablation studies, and benchmarking against PCA-based statistical shape models and point-based generative frameworks. Results show that synchronized spectral modeling achieves reconstruction and generative performance comparable to or exceeding spatial approaches, while maintaining compactness and geometric interpretability. Together, the compact synchronized coefficients and low-dimensional learning space make the framework particularly suitable for limited datasets, as often encountered in dental and medical domains, and applicable in real-world scenarios where guaranteeing consistent connectivity across shapes from various clinics is unrealistic.

AI.tldr: ToothForge is a deep spectral generative framework for dental crown modeling that uses synchronized spectral embeddings to learn compact, intrinsic representations from limited datasets, achieving competitive performance against spatial methods while maintaining geometric interpretability.
AI.motivation: Dental crown morphology modeling is crucial for diagnosis and restoration, but available 3D shape datasets are typically small, making it difficult to train effective generative models. Existing approaches often require consistent mesh connectivity across samples, which is unrealistic in clinical practice.
AI.method: ToothForge operates in the spectral domain using synchronized spectral embeddings of 3D tooth shapes. It mitigates Laplace-Beltrami eigenbasis instability via spectral synchronization, enabling consistent learning across samples with varying connectivity in a low-dimensional latent space.
AI.result: Synchronized spectral modeling achieves reconstruction and generative performance comparable to or exceeding spatial approaches (PCA-based and point-based models), while offering compactness and geometric interpretability. It performs well on limited datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework's compactness and low-dimensional learning space make it well-suited for small dental/medical datasets and real-world scenarios where consistent mesh connectivity across clinics cannot be guaranteed.

title: Wavelet-Optimized Pseudo-3D Accelerated Diffusion Model for Truncated Computed Laminography
comment: 17 pages, 11 figures, 4 tables. Under review at NDT&E International
summary: Computed Laminography (CL) is a key technology for the nondestructive testing of large plate-shaped objects. However, field-of-view (FOV) limitations inevitably lead to truncation of projected data, an ill-posed inverse problem that causes severe reconstruction artifacts. Existing deep learning methods typically rely on 2D architectures that lack rigorous data consistency constraints. Furthermore, they conventionally confine artifact removal strictly to the FOV, discarding potentially recoverable information outside it. To overcome these limitations, we first introduce a comprehensive CL FOV analysis, categorizing the space into data-complete, data-incomplete, and data-free regions. By extending our reconstruction target to encompass the data-incomplete region, we significantly expand the effective imaging range and enhance scanning efficiency. To achieve this, we propose a novel wavelet-optimized pseudo-3D accelerated diffusion model for CL truncation reconstruction (CL-DM). Our method utilizes a standard 2D diffusion model for slice aggregation, combined with a 3D model-based iterative reconstruction (MBIR) method to ensure strict data consistency. To mitigate inter-slice discontinuities, we introduce wavelet regularization along the z-direction, paired with a translation-invariant (TI) mechanism and a low-frequency preservation strategy. Finally, we introduce a 3D fast sampling architecture, significantly accelerating inference speed. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that CL-DM is superior in effectively eliminating truncation artifacts and restoring high-fidelity, continuous 3D structures.

AI.tldr: A wavelet-optimized pseudo-3D diffusion model (CL-DM) for computed laminography that extends reconstruction beyond truncated field-of-view, using 2D diffusion for slice aggregation, 3D MBIR for data consistency, and wavelet regularization to eliminate truncation artifacts and restore continuous 3D structures.
AI.motivation: Field-of-view limitations in Computed Laminography cause projection data truncation, leading to severe reconstruction artifacts. Existing deep learning methods lack rigorous data consistency constraints and confine artifact removal to the FOV, discarding recoverable information outside it.
AI.method: 1) Categorizes space into data-complete, data-incomplete, and data-free regions. 2) Extends reconstruction target to the data-incomplete region. 3) Proposes a wavelet-optimized pseudo-3D accelerated diffusion model (CL-DM): combines 2D diffusion for slice aggregation with 3D MBIR for data consistency, uses wavelet regularization along z-direction with translation-invariant mechanism and low-frequency preservation, plus a 3D fast sampling architecture for acceleration.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate CL-DM effectively eliminates truncation artifacts and restores high-fidelity, continuous 3D structures, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CL-DM significantly expands the effective imaging range and enhances scanning efficiency by recovering information in data-incomplete regions, representing a superior approach for CL truncation reconstruction.

title: Accelerated Likelihood Maximization for Diffusion-based Versatile Content Generation
summary: Generating diverse, coherent, and plausible content from partially given inputs remains a fundamental challenge for diffusion models. Existing approaches face clear limitations: training-based approaches offer strong task-specific results but require costly computation, and they generalize poorly across tasks. Training-free approaches offer better efficiency, but they do not explicitly optimize over unobserved variables, leading to globally inconsistent results. To address these limitations, we introduce Accelerated Likelihood Maximization (ALM), a novel training-free sampling strategy integrated into the reverse diffusion process that significantly extends the applicability of diffusion models beyond simple generation tasks. Unlike previous methods that implicitly influence missing regions through pre-generated region constraints, we directly optimize the unobserved region during the sampling process, enabling globally coherent and plausible generation. Furthermore, we incorporate an acceleration strategy that significantly improves computational efficiency without sacrificing performance. Experimental results demonstrate that ALM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in various data domains and tasks, establishing a powerful paradigm for versatile content generation.

AI.tldr: ALM is a training-free sampling method for diffusion models that directly optimizes unobserved variables to generate globally coherent content, outperforming existing approaches across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based generation methods face a trade-off: training-based approaches are costly and poorly generalizable, while training-free methods lack explicit optimization over unobserved variables, causing global inconsistency.
AI.method: ALM (Accelerated Likelihood Maximization) integrates a training-free sampling strategy that directly optimizes unobserved regions during the reverse diffusion process, coupled with an acceleration mechanism for efficiency.
AI.result: ALM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple data domains and tasks in terms of diversity, coherence, and plausibility.
AI.conclusion: ALM establishes a powerful paradigm for versatile content generation by overcoming the global inconsistency problem of training-free methods while avoiding the computational costs and poor generalization of training-based approaches.

title: Bridging Video Understanding and Generation in a Unified Framework
summary: Recently, unified image generation and understanding have been extensively explored. However, extending such unified modeling paradigms to the video domain remains largely underexplored. A central challenge is that video understanding favors compact, discriminative semantic representations, whereas video generation requires dense signals that preserve visual details and temporal coherence. Videos naturally capture both spatial semantics and temporal dynamics, making them a more suitable modality for unified multimodal modeling compared to static images. In this paper, we propose Vega, a unified framework that bridges video understanding and generation. Vega leverages a shared vocabulary to jointly model text and visual representations and employs a hybrid architecture combining autoregressive (AR) prediction with diffusion-based rendering. Specifically, the AR model focuses on predicting semantically meaningful visual tokens for keyframes, providing a structured representation that guides the diffusion module in rendering dense, high-resolution video frames. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Vega achieves strong performance on video generation benchmarks such as VBench and video understanding benchmarks like VideoMME.

AI.tldr: Vega is a unified framework that bridges video generation and understanding by combining autoregressive prediction for semantic keyframes with diffusion-based rendering for high-resolution video output, achieving strong results on both generation and understanding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: While unified image generation/understanding has been explored, extending this to video remains underexplored. Video is a more suitable modality than static images for unified modeling because it captures both spatial semantics and temporal dynamics. The key challenge is balancing compact semantic representations (needed for understanding) with dense visual details (needed for generation).
AI.method: Vega uses a shared vocabulary to jointly model text and visual representations, along with a hybrid architecture: (1) an autoregressive model predicts semantically meaningful visual tokens for keyframes, (2) a diffusion module renders dense, high-resolution video frames guided by the structured AR representations.
AI.result: Achieves strong performance on video generation benchmarks (VBench) and video understanding benchmarks (VideoMME).
AI.conclusion: The hybrid AR+diffusion approach effectively bridges video understanding and generation within a single unified framework, validating that video is a promising modality for unified multimodal modeling.

title: Patient-Level Elbow Abnormality Detection: Leakage-Aware Evaluation of Learned Preprocessing, Calibration, and Triage-Oriented Operating Points
summary: In this study, we examine learned preprocessing pipelines in the context of triage-oriented orthopedic abnormality detection task using elbow radiographs from MURA dataset. The evaluation focuses on patient-level detection of musculoskeletal abnormalities under a leakage-aware protocol. We compare multiple preprocessing pipelines, with and without a lightweight DnCNN module as a learned preprocessing component, to assess their impact on discrimination and calibration. Performance is assessed using discrimination metrics (AUROC, PR-AUC), calibration measures (ECE, Brier score), and validation-selected operating point analysis targeting high specificity. Results show that differences across preprocessing strategies are modest and configuration-dependent, with no consistent discrimination advantage over the raw-input DenseNet121 baseline. The raw and diverse inputs combined with the DnCNN front-end showed reduced ECE and Brier score, while CLAHE combined with DnCNN did not improve calibration. Overall, the results suggest that under patient-level evaluation, preprocessing gains are modest and configuration-dependent; the raw-input DenseNet121 baseline remains competitive throughout, and no tested preprocessing strategy produced a consistent discrimination advantage across all metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates learned preprocessing pipelines (including DnCNN) for elbow abnormality detection using the MURA dataset. Under patient-level, leakage-aware evaluation, preprocessing yields modest, configuration-dependent improvements, with raw-input DenseNet121 remaining competitive across all metrics.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether learned preprocessing pipelines (e.g., DnCNN) improve discrimination and calibration for triage-oriented orthopedic abnormality detection compared to raw-input baselines, under a proper leakage-aware patient-level evaluation protocol.
AI.method: Used MURA elbow radiographs, compared multiple preprocessing pipelines (with/without lightweight DnCNN module), evaluated under patient-level, leakage-aware protocol; assessed via AUROC, PR-AUC, ECE, Brier score, and high-specificity operating point analysis.
AI.result: Preprocessing gains are modest and configuration-dependent; raw and diverse inputs + DnCNN reduced ECE and Brier score, but CLAHE + DnCNN did not improve calibration. No preprocessing strategy consistently outperformed raw-input DenseNet121 baseline across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: Learned preprocessing offers no consistent discrimination advantage over the raw-input DenseNet121 baseline under rigorous patient-level evaluation; benefits are modest and highly configuration-dependent.

title: RCL-Mamba: A Dual-domain State Space Model for Measurement-oriented Image Restoration in Rotational Sparse-View Scanning Computed Laminography
summary: Rotational Scanning Computed Laminography (RCL) is widely utilized for the Non-Destructive Testing(NDT) of large planar components. However, to facilitate rapid inspection, continuous sparse-view scanning is often employed, where the angular integration effect during exposure induces rotational blur in the projection domain. Furthermore, the data incompleteness inherent in sparse sampling manifests as sparse artifacts in the reconstructed image domain. To address these cross-domain degradations, this paper proposes RCL-Mamba, a measurement-oriented dual-domain State Space Model (SSM)-based image restoration network. The framework adopts a cascaded joint processing strategy: it first corrects the rotational blur in the projection domain and subsequently suppresses the sparse artifacts in the image domain. Additionally, we design a Mamba-CNN dual-branch module to adaptively balance large-scale blur correction with local detail recovery. Evaluations on both simulated datasets and real-world Printed Circuit Board (PCB) scans demonstrate that RCL-Mamba outperforms existing baselines in blur removal, artifact suppression, and structural preservation. Line-profile-based structural measurement further verifies that the proposed method better preserves via/pad boundaries and slender trace profiles. Crucially, by reducing the required scanning views from 512 to 64, our method enhances inspection efficiency by approximately 8-fold without compromising reconstruction quality, offering a robust measurement-oriented restoration solution for high-throughput RCL inspection with improved structural measurement fidelity.

AI.tldr: RCL-Mamba is a dual-domain state space model that corrects rotational blur in projections and sparse artifacts in reconstructions for sparse-view RCL, achieving 8× speedup in PCB inspection without quality loss.
AI.motivation: To address cross-domain degradations (rotational blur from continuous scanning in projection domain + sparse artifacts from undersampling in image domain) in rapid sparse-view RCL for NDT of large planar components like PCBs.
AI.method: A cascaded dual-domain SSM-based restoration network (RCL-Mamba) with: (1) projection-domain rotational blur correction; (2) image-domain sparse artifact suppression; (3) Mamba-CNN dual-branch module balancing global blur correction and local detail recovery.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in blur removal, artifact suppression, and structural preservation on simulated and real PCB data; reduces required scanning views from 512 to 64 (8× efficiency gain) without quality degradation; better preserves via/pad boundaries and trace profiles.
AI.conclusion: RCL-Mamba provides a robust measurement-oriented restoration solution enabling high-throughput RCL inspection with improved structural measurement fidelity at 8× fewer scanning views.

title: Language-Assisted Super-Resolution from Real-World Low-Resolution Patches
summary: Single image super-resolution aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) inputs. Training SR models typically requires paired HR-LR data, which is difficult to obtain in reality. As a result, most methods synthesize LR images by artificially degrading HR images with handcrafted kernels or camera ISP adjustments. However, these synthetic degradations fail to capture the complexity of real LR images, leading to poor generalization in practice. To address this, we observe that even within a single high-quality image, regions at different depths exhibit varying resolutions, where distant regions act as LR patches and closer ones as HR patches. This allows the extraction of real, degradation-induced LR patches from real images. Since these LR patches lack paired HR counterparts, we propose LA-SR (Language Assistant for SR), a novel framework for unpaired SR. The key idea of LA-SR is to redefine unpaired SR in the language space, using vision-language models to bridge the LR-HR gap. LA-SR projects images into a semantically rich space representing both content and quality, and applies two language-guided losses: linguistic content loss to preserve semantic fidelity, and linguistic quality loss to enhance perceptual realism. With this alignment, LA-SR effectively super-resolves real LR inputs, producing realistic outputs that overcome the limitations of synthetic-data-trained methods.

AI.tldr: LA-SR proposes an unpaired super-resolution framework that extracts real LR patches from high-quality images based on depth, then uses vision-language models to bridge the LR-HR gap via language-guided losses for content and quality.
AI.motivation: Existing SR models rely on synthetic HR-LR pairs that fail to capture real-world degradation complexity, leading to poor generalization. Real paired data is difficult to obtain.
AI.method: Extracts real LR patches from single HQ images using depth-variance; redefines unpaired SR in language space via vision-language models; applies linguistic content and quality losses.
AI.result: LA-SR effectively super-resolves real LR inputs, producing realistic outputs that overcome limitations of synthetic-data-trained methods.
AI.conclusion: Using language space alignment for unpaired SR is a viable alternative to synthetic degradation approaches, enabling better generalization to real-world images.

title: Domain Adaptive Object Detection via Dual-Stream Bilevel-Cycle Optimization
summary: Cycle self-training (CST) breaks the shared classifier assumption of the standard self-training framework, which is effective for unsupervised domain adaptation and exploits unlabeled target data by training with target pseudo-labels. CST introduces a target classifier and employs an inner-outer loop updating strategy, addressing the issue of unreliable pseudo-labels and enabling pseudo-labels to generalize across domains. Despite its success in image classification, extending CST to object detection faces three main challenges. First, the upper bound of CST in object detection is constrained by three types of unreliable pseudo-labels, such as classification error alone, localization error alone, and their combination. Second, since object detection involves detecting multiple target objects, directly applying CST leads to training insta bility. Third, a wider numerical range of regression coordinates leads to exploding losses. To this end, we apply CST to both classification and regression and propose the Dual-Stream Bilevel-Cycle Optimization framework. Specifically, we construct CST upon Mean Teacher to prevent training instability and use extra normalization to map the regression bounding box into a standardized space, effectively addressing exploding losses. Also, we provide a theoretical derivation of the regression bound. Extensive experiments across four cross domain standard scenarios demonstrate that our framework achieves considerable results.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Cycle Self-Training (CST) from image classification to object detection by addressing three challenges: unreliable pseudo-labels (classification/localization errors), training instability from multiple objects, and exploding regression losses. The proposed Dual-Stream Bilevel-Cycle Optimization framework integrates CST with Mean Teacher, extra normalization, and theoretical regression bounds, achieving strong results across four cross-domain scenarios.
AI.motivation: To extend Cycle Self-Training (CST), which is effective for unsupervised domain adaptation in image classification, to object detection by overcoming three key challenges: unreliable pseudo-labels (classification error, localization error, and both combined), training instability from detecting multiple objects, and exploding losses from wide numerical ranges in regression coordinates.
AI.method: Proposes Dual-Stream Bilevel-Cycle Optimization framework: (1) Applies CST to both classification and regression branches; (2) Constructs CST upon Mean Teacher to stabilize training; (3) Uses extra normalization to map regression bounding boxes into a standardized space to prevent exploding losses; (4) Provides theoretical derivation of the regression bound.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across four cross-domain standard scenarios demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves considerable results in unsupervised domain adaptation for object detection.
AI.conclusion: The Dual-Stream Bilevel-Cycle Optimization successfully adapts Cycle Self-Training to object detection by addressing unreliable pseudo-labels, training instability, and exploding losses, validated across multiple cross-domain benchmarks.

title: MAPE: Defending Against Transferable Adversarial Attacks Using Multi-Source Adversarial Perturbations Elimination
summary: Neural networks are vulnerable to meticulously crafted adversarial examples, leading to high-confidence misclassifications in image classification tasks. Due to their consistency with regular input patterns and the absence of reliance on the target model and its output information, transferable adversarial attacks exhibit a notably high stealthiness and detection difficulty, making them a significant focus of defense. In this work, we propose a deep learning defense known as multi-source adversarial perturbations elimination (MAPE) to counter diverse transferable attacks. MAPE comprises the single-source adversarial perturbation elimination (SAPE) mechanism and the pre-trained models probabilistic scheduling algorithm (PPSA). SAPE utilizes a thoughtfully designed channel-attention U-Net as the defense model and employs adversarial examples generated by a pre-trained model (e.g., ResNet) for its training, thereby enabling the elimination of known adversarial perturbations. PPSA introduces model difference quantification and negative momentum to strategically schedule multiple pre-trained models, thereby maximizing the differences among adversarial examples during the defense model's training and enhancing its robustness in eliminating adversarial perturbations. MAPE effectively eliminates adversarial perturbations in various adversarial examples, providing a robust defense against attacks from different substitute models. In a black-box attack scenario utilizing ResNet-34 as the target model, our approach achieves average defense rates of over 95.1\% on CIFAR-10 and over 71.5\% on Mini-ImageNet, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: MAPE is a deep learning defense against transferable adversarial attacks that uses a channel-attention U-Net and a probabilistic scheduling algorithm to eliminate multi-source adversarial perturbations, achieving over 95.1% defense rate on CIFAR-10 and 71.5% on Mini-ImageNet.
AI.motivation: Transferable adversarial attacks are highly stealthy and difficult to detect since they don't rely on target model information, making effective defense against them a critical challenge.
AI.method: Two components: (1) SAPE uses a channel-attention U-Net trained on adversarial examples from a single pre-trained model to eliminate known perturbations; (2) PPSA quantifies model differences and uses negative momentum to schedule multiple pre-trained models, maximizing diversity of adversarial examples during training to improve robustness.
AI.result: In black-box attacks against ResNet-34, MAPE achieves average defense rates of >95.1% on CIFAR-10 and >71.5% on Mini-ImageNet, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: MAPE effectively eliminates adversarial perturbations from diverse sources and provides robust defense against attacks from different substitute models.

title: MS-Resampler: Multi-Scope Visual Resampling for Efficient Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) typically employ resampling-based projectors to transform dense visual features into a compact token sequence for language modeling. Most existing resamplers adopt a single, fixed aggregation scope via global cross-attention, which can blur fine-grained local evidence and limit the ability to capture both local details and global context within a fixed token budget. In this work, we propose MS-Resampler, a multi-scope visual resampling framework for MLLMs. MS-Resampler instantiates multiple scope-specific resamplers by injecting explicit spatial scope priors into the resampling attention, enabling each branch to aggregate visual information at a particular granularity from local to global. The outputs of these scope-specific resamplers are then adaptively fused to produce the final visual representations for language modeling. Extensive experiments on ten public multimodal benchmarks show that MS-Resampler consistently improves visual understanding and multimodal reasoning over conventional single-scope resamplers, while introducing only minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MS-Resampler, a multi-scope visual resampling framework for MLLMs that uses multiple scope-specific resamplers with explicit spatial priors to capture both local details and global context, outperforming single-scope resamplers on multimodal benchmarks with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing resamplers in MLLMs use a single, fixed aggregation scope via global cross-attention, which blurs fine-grained local evidence and limits the ability to capture both local details and global context within a fixed token budget.
AI.method: MS-Resampler instantiates multiple scope-specific resamplers by injecting explicit spatial scope priors into the resampling attention, enabling each branch to aggregate visual information at a particular granularity from local to global. The outputs are then adaptively fused for language modeling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ten public multimodal benchmarks show consistent improvements in visual understanding and multimodal reasoning over conventional single-scope resamplers, with minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Multi-scope visual resampling effectively captures both local and global visual information, improving MLLM performance without significant computational cost.

title: One Video, One World: Turning Monocular Video into Physical 4D Scenes
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026. Project Page: https://OneVideoOneWorld.github.io/
summary: We introduce \textbf{OVOW}, the first training-free system that reconstructs \emph{instance-level, simulation-ready} 4D mesh scenes from a single monocular video. Recent 4D reconstruction achieves impressive rendering quality, but its outputs (\eg, implicit fields, Gaussian primitives, or point clouds) lack the watertight topology, instance separation, and standardized physical interfaces required by physics simulators and embodied AI. OVOW closes this gap with a four-stage pipeline: a vision-language model discovers, labels, and motion-classifies all instances; category-aware reconstruction yields per-instance meshes for rigid objects and topology-consistent mesh sequences for deformable ones; an iterative render-match-optimize procedure recovers metric scale and 6-DoF pose trajectories; and physics-grounded assembly enforces ground contact and inter-object support. Crucially, we model all motion, rigid and non-rigid, through direct vertex deformation without category-specific priors or skeleton rigging, producing watertight mesh scenes ready for downstream physics simulation and editing. We further establish the first benchmark for \emph{structured Video-to-4D} evaluation, with metrics for geometric correctness, instance separation, and physical plausibility beyond visual fidelity; the same pipeline doubles as a scalable engine for \emph{synthesizing} paired video-to-4D simulation data for future 4D world models and embodied AI. Across two synthetic benchmarks (static and 4D), OVOW attains the best overall layout and geometry accuracy and the lowest photometric and semantic error among all baselines, and on monocular video runs one to two orders of magnitude faster than the baselines, while downstream physics simulation confirms its physical stability.

AI.tldr: OVOW is a training-free system that reconstructs instance-level, simulation-ready 4D mesh scenes from a single monocular video, using a four-stage pipeline involving vision-language discovery, category-aware reconstruction, render-match-optimize pose recovery, and physics-grounded assembly, achieving superior geometry, layout, and physical stability while being significantly faster than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D reconstruction methods produce implicit fields, Gaussian primitives, or point clouds that lack watertight topology, instance separation, and standardized physical interfaces needed by physics simulators and embodied AI. OVOW aims to close this gap by producing structured, simulation-ready 4D meshes.
AI.method: A four-stage pipeline: (1) vision-language model discovers, labels, and motion-classifies instances; (2) category-aware reconstruction yields per-instance meshes (rigid) and topology-consistent mesh sequences (deformable); (3) iterative render-match-optimize recovers metric scale and 6-DoF pose trajectories; (4) physics-grounded assembly enforces ground contact and inter-object support. All motion is modeled via direct vertex deformation without category-specific priors or skeleton rigging.
AI.result: OVOW achieves the best layout and geometry accuracy, lowest photometric and semantic error on synthetic benchmarks, runs 1-2 orders of magnitude faster than baselines on monocular video, and demonstrates physical stability in downstream physics simulation.
AI.conclusion: OVOW establishes the first benchmark for structured Video-to-4D evaluation with metrics beyond visual fidelity, and serves as a scalable engine for synthesizing paired video-to-4D simulation data for future 4D world models and embodied AI.

title: Visual Semantic Entropy: Do Vision Language Models Recognize Visual Ambiguity?
summary: Vision-language models can produce confident answers on visually ambiguous inputs, resulting in biased predictions. Common entropy-based methods, such as Semantic Entropy (SE), rely on output diversity. Yet our analysis shows that overconfident visual embeddings suppress output diversity under stochastic decoding, causing SE to underestimate uncertainty in such cases. Recent methods instead probe output diversity through input perturbations, including textual paraphrasing or joint text-image perturbations, and show improved performance. We study these approaches and reveals that the resulting variability is often dominated by textual changes rather than visual evidence, causing uncertainty estimates to reflect prompt sensitivity rather than visual ambiguity. We therefore propose Visual Semantic Entropy (VSE), which perturbs only the image to probe nearby visual variations while keeping the text query fixed. VSE measures uncertainty by clustering generated answers into semantic prototypes and computing the mass-weighted dispersion among them. Extensive evaluation across five modern vision-language models and five diverse VQA benchmarks demonstrates that VSE effectively captures visual ambiguity, establishing a new state-of-the-art for VLM uncertainty estimation.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that existing entropy-based uncertainty methods for VLMs fail under visual ambiguity due to overconfident embeddings and textual dominance. The authors propose Visual Semantic Entropy (VSE), which perturbs only images while keeping text fixed and clusters generated answers into semantic prototypes, achieving SOTA uncertainty estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty estimation methods (e.g., Semantic Entropy) fail for vision-language models on visually ambiguous inputs because overconfident visual embeddings suppress output diversity, and perturbation-based methods are dominated by textual rather than visual changes.
AI.method: VSE perturbs only the image while keeping the text query fixed, generates multiple answers, clusters them into semantic prototypes, and computes mass-weighted dispersion among prototypes to measure uncertainty.
AI.result: VSE achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty estimation across five modern VLMs and five diverse VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Probing visual variations via image-only perturbations while clustering semantic prototypes effectively captures visual ambiguity for VLM uncertainty estimation.

title: Temporal Preservation over Processing: Diagnosing and Designing Spatiotemporal Single-Stage Video Detectors
summary: Single-stage video object detectors are increasingly deployed in time-critical applications, yet it remains unclear whether these models genuinely reason over temporal context or merely exploit a single informative frame-a gap hidden by standard metrics, which reward correct predictions regardless of how they are reached. We address this from two complementary directions: first, we propose TemporalLens, a model-agnostic diagnostic framework probing temporal dependence through controlled perturbations, structured occlusions, temporal shuffling, redundancy injection, and resolution degradation, revealing whether a detector actually uses information across time. Applied to stacked-frame 2D detectors and our YOLO-3D architecture, it exposes behavioural differences invisible to mAP: stacked 2D models collapse when the target frame is removed, while spatiotemporal models recover predictions from earlier frames, a signature of real temporal reliance. Second, we detail YOLO-3D, a modular real-time spatiotemporal detector built on YOLOv8, and show that simply preserving temporal depth through the backbone is the dominant performance driver (+3.7 pp mAP@50 at 32 frames averaged across scales). Together, the diagnostics and architecture turn "does this detector reason over time?" into a measurable, actionable question.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TemporalLens, a diagnostic framework to test whether video object detectors truly use temporal context, and introduces YOLO-3D, a real-time spatiotemporal detector. Findings show stacked 2D models collapse without the target frame, while YOLO-3D recovers predictions from earlier frames; preserving temporal depth through the backbone is the key performance driver (+3.7 pp mAP@50).
AI.motivation: Standard metrics like mAP reward correct predictions regardless of how they are reached, making it unclear whether single-stage video detectors genuinely reason over temporal context or simply exploit a single informative frame.
AI.method: Proposes TemporalLens, a model-agnostic diagnostic framework using controlled perturbations (structured occlusions, temporal shuffling, redundancy injection, resolution degradation). Also introduces YOLO-3D, a modular real-time spatiotemporal detector built on YOLOv8.
AI.result: Stacked 2D models collapse when target frame is removed; spatiotemporal models (YOLO-3D) recover predictions from earlier frames. Preserving temporal depth through the backbone yields +3.7 pp mAP@50 at 32 frames averaged across scales.
AI.conclusion: The diagnostics and architecture turn "does this detector reason over time?" into a measurable, actionable question.

title: No Prompt, No Leaks: A Robust Generative Steganography Framework via Prompt-Free Diffusion
summary: Generative image steganography synthesizes stego images directly from secret information to achieve inherent security advantages. Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have recently emerged as a fundamental image steganography framework that modulates secret latent representations with text prompts. Limited by the inflexibility of text prompts, these methods still struggle to generate high-quality stego images and accurately recover secret images. In this work, we propose a prompt-free diffusion image steganography framework that integrates style semantic priors to control more robust and reliable stego image generation. Specifically, a Cascaded Affine Coupling Module (CACM) establishes a bijective, deterministic mapping between a secret image and its latent representation. Then, style semantics are integrated into the diffusion process to control latent representation and ensure visual imperceptibility in the generated stego images. To mitigate trajectory deviations stemming from the unconditioned reverse process, a predictor-corrector mechanism is introduced to iteratively refine the generation trajectory via feedback from the current and predicted next states. Extensive experimental results show that the proposed method achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in terms of security, secret image reconstruction accuracy and controllability.

AI.tldr: A prompt-free diffusion image steganography method using style semantic priors and a Cascaded Affine Coupling Module (CACM) for robust stego image generation, with a predictor-corrector mechanism to refine generation trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing LDM-based steganography methods are limited by the inflexibility of text prompts, struggling to generate high-quality stego images and accurately recover secret images.
AI.method: (1) Cascaded Affine Coupling Module (CACM) for bijective mapping between secret image and latent representation; (2) Integration of style semantic priors into diffusion process to control latent representation; (3) Predictor-corrector mechanism to iteratively refine the generation trajectory.
AI.result: Competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in security, secret image reconstruction accuracy, and controllability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prompt-free framework with style semantic priors effectively addresses text prompt inflexibility, improving stego image quality and secret recovery accuracy.

title: Temporal Training Strategies for Left Atrium and Left Atrial Appendage Segmentation in Dynamic Contrast 4DCT
summary: Dynamic contrast-enhanced cardiac CT enables time-resolved analysis of contrast filling and washout in the left atrium (LA) and left atrial appendage (LAA), with potential applications for assessing blood stasis in atrial fibrillation (AF). Accurate segmentation across all frames is required for such analysis but is challenging due to large temporal contrast variations and the use of a single annotation per registered sequence. This creates a trade-off between training for robustness and limiting label noise. In this study, we investigate how temporal training-set design affects nnUNet-based segmentation of the LA and LAA in dynamic 4DCT. We compare training using a minimal two-frame dataset reflecting standard clinical practice, a physiologically selected subset of frames, and the full 27-frame sequence. We further evaluate the impact of foreground-based normalization. Training with all frames yielded the best performance in early low-contrast phases. However, the physiologically selected subset achieved comparable performance from the filling phase onward. Applying normalization parameters derived from the full dataset improved performance of reduced datasets in low-contrast frames, but did not fully close the gap. These findings highlight the importance of temporal diversity in training data for robust segmentation in dynamic CT, while indicating that carefully selected frame subsets may provide an effective trade-off between performance and efficiency for downstream applications.

AI.tldr: This study examines how temporal training-set design affects nnUNet-based segmentation of the left atrium and left atrial appendage in dynamic 4DCT, finding that training with all frames yields best performance in low-contrast phases, while a physiologically selected subset achieves comparable results from the filling phase onward, with foreground-based normalization partially helping reduced datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of accurate segmentation across all temporal frames in dynamic contrast-enhanced cardiac CT for assessing blood stasis in atrial fibrillation, given the trade-off between training for robustness and limiting label noise.
AI.method: Compared nnUNet-based segmentation performance using three temporal training-set designs: minimal two-frame dataset (standard clinical practice), physiologically selected subset, and full 27-frame sequence; also evaluated foreground-based normalization.
AI.result: Full-frame training performed best in early low-contrast phases; physiologically selected subset matched performance from filling phase onward; normalization from full dataset improved reduced datasets in low-contrast frames but didn't fully close the gap.
AI.conclusion: Temporal diversity in training data is crucial for robust dynamic CT segmentation, but carefully selected frame subsets offer an effective trade-off between performance and efficiency for downstream applications.

title: Towards a foundational model for recognising diastematic Gregorian notation
summary: Optical recognition of Gregorian notation has recently been attempted with end-to-end methods, with four datasets introduced. However, each of these datasets is in a different encoding. We design a common encoding based on the S-GABC proposal, convert all four datasets to this common encoding, and train a shared end-to-end foundational model for diastematic Gregorian notation that establishes a new state of the art across all four datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a common encoding based on S-GABC for Gregorian notation, converts four existing datasets to it, and trains a shared end-to-end model that achieves state-of-the-art results across all datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets for optical recognition of Gregorian notation are encoded differently, preventing cross-dataset learning and limiting model generalization.
AI.method: Design a common encoding based on S-GABC, convert all four datasets to this encoding, and train a shared end-to-end foundational model.
AI.result: Establishes a new state of the art across all four datasets.
AI.conclusion: A unified encoding and shared model approach can improve recognition performance across diverse Gregorian notation datasets.

title: AeroVerse-SatAgent: UAV-Satellite Collaborative Spatial Reasoning Inspired by the Dual Visual Pathway Theory of Cognitive Neuroscience
summary: With the rapid advancement of aerospace embodied intelligence, enabling Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to autonomously understand and reason about complex environments has become increasingly important. However, existing UAV-based spatial reasoning approaches face critical limitations: single-view perception renders them vulnerable to occlusions and perspective distortions, while most VLMs lack explicit geometric modeling, relying on semantic cues and yielding inconsistent reasoning under viewpoint and scale variations. To address these challenges, we propose SatAgent, a UAV-Satellite collaborative spatial reasoning model inspired by the dual-pathway mechanism of the human visual system. By jointly leveraging satellite and UAV perspectives, SatAgent enables robust, accurate reasoning in complex urban environments. We first introduce a Geometric-Aware 3D Reconstruction Encoder that elevates 2D UAV features into explicit 3D spatial representations. Next, we design a multi-view topology-semantic alignment module integrating cross-view features within a unified BEV coordinate system. We further introduce a multi-view consistency loss encouraging viewpoint-invariant representations. Finally, we construct SatAgent-SR130K, the first large-scale UAV-Satellite collaborative multi-view spatial reasoning dataset. Experiments show SatAgent outperforms state-of-the-art general-purpose foundation models and specialized spatial reasoning models by 25.91\% and 11.69\%, respectively, across diverse tasks, achieving particularly high accuracy in complex geometric relationship reasoning.

AI.tldr: SatAgent is a UAV-Satellite collaborative spatial reasoning model that uses dual-pathway visual processing and geometric-aware 3D reconstruction to overcome occlusion, perspective distortion, and viewpoint inconsistency in UAV-based spatial reasoning, outperforming existing models by 11-26%.
AI.motivation: Existing UAV spatial reasoning is limited by single-view perception (occlusions, perspective distortions) and VLMs lacking explicit geometric modeling, yielding inconsistent reasoning under viewpoint and scale variations.
AI.method: SatAgent introduces (1) a Geometric-Aware 3D Reconstruction Encoder to lift 2D UAV features to 3D, (2) a multi-view topology-semantic alignment module unifying cross-view features in BEV coordinates, (3) a multi-view consistency loss for viewpoint-invariant representations, and (4) the SatAgent-SR130K dataset for training.
AI.result: Outperforms general-purpose foundation models by 25.91% and specialized spatial reasoning models by 11.69% across diverse tasks, with particularly high accuracy in complex geometric relationship reasoning.
AI.conclusion: UAV-Satellite collaboration with explicit geometric modeling and viewpoint-invariant learning significantly improves spatial reasoning in complex urban environments.

title: Think While You Map: Asynchronous Vision-Language Agents for Incremental 3D Scene Graphs
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D scene graph methods typically operate in two stages: first reconstruct, then enrich with vision-language models, leaving the graph unqueryable during exploration. We argue that this sequential coupling is unnecessary and propose an asynchronous architecture in which lightweight online mapping runs concurrently with heavyweight semantic refinement. A probabilistic voxel-based backbone maintains stable object identities incrementally, while background VLM agents progressively enrich the graph. This framework resolves duplicate object tracks through semantic loop closure, attaches fine-grained visual attributes and derives spatial relations between objects. A multi-target frame scheduler amortizes VLM cost by selecting a small set of informative frames that jointly cover multiple targets. The resulting scene graph is queryable during exploration and grows in semantic richness over time. Our method matches or outperforms existing open-vocabulary 3D scene graph methods on semantic segmentation (ScanNet, Replica) and surpasses the prior state-of-the-art across three visual grounding benchmarks (Sr3D+, Nr3D, ScanRefer) by 15.3 to 18.8 A@0.25. Project page: https://denizbickici.github.io/thinkgraphs/

AI.tldr: Proposes an asynchronous architecture for open-vocabulary 3D scene graphs that runs lightweight online mapping concurrently with heavyweight VLM-based semantic refinement, enabling queryable graphs during exploration and achieving state-of-the-art results on visual grounding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing open-vocabulary 3D scene graph methods use a sequential two-stage pipeline (reconstruct first, then enrich with VLMs), which leaves the graph unqueryable during exploration. The authors argue this sequential coupling is unnecessary.
AI.method: An asynchronous architecture with (1) a probabilistic voxel-based backbone for incremental mapping and stable object identities, (2) background VLM agents for progressive semantic refinement, (3) semantic loop closure to resolve duplicate object tracks, (4) fine-grained visual attributes and spatial relations, and (5) a multi-target frame scheduler that selects informative frames to amortize VLM cost.
AI.result: Matches or outperforms existing methods on semantic segmentation (ScanNet, Replica); surpasses prior SOTA on Sr3D+, Nr3D, and ScanRefer visual grounding benchmarks by 15.3 to 18.8 A@0.25.
AI.conclusion: The asynchronous architecture enables queryable scene graphs during exploration with growing semantic richness over time, while outperforming prior methods on multiple benchmarks.

title: DrivingDepth: Sparse-Prompted Pixel-wise Scale Correction for Driving Depth Estimation
summary: Dense depth estimation for autonomous driving faces a geometry-scale conflict: depth foundation models deliver pixel-aligned dense visual geometry without reliable metric scale, while projected LiDAR provides metric anchors that are sparse, noisy, and misaligned with image structures. Existing sparse-prompted methods incorporate LiDAR by regenerating depth from scratch, overriding the foundation model's coherent geometry and producing structural artifacts on visually continuous surfaces. Our key insight is that foundation models already capture geometrically coherent relative depth; no additional surface structure learning is required-only a per-pixel scale factor mapping relative geometry to metric coordinates. Based on this, we propose DrivingDepth, which treats sparse LiDAR as geometric prompts that locally calibrate a frozen foundation prior through residual pixel-wise scale correction, preserving dense visual geometry by construction. On nuScenes with 4-frame surround-view input, DrivingDepth achieves an AbsRel of 11.19 and an EdgeCR of 5.741, outperforming MapAnything (11.99/1.914) by simultaneously delivering SOTA metric accuracy and geometric consistency.

AI.tldr: DrivingDepth proposes using sparse LiDAR as geometric prompts to locally calibrate a frozen depth foundation model via per-pixel scale correction, achieving SOTA metric accuracy and geometric consistency for autonomous driving depth estimation.
AI.motivation: Dense depth estimation faces a geometry-scale conflict: depth foundation models provide pixel-aligned relative geometry without metric scale, while LiDAR offers metric anchors that are sparse, noisy, and misaligned with image structures. Existing methods override foundation geometry, causing structural artifacts.
AI.method: DrivingDepth treats sparse LiDAR as geometric prompts to locally calibrate a frozen depth foundation prior through per-pixel residual scale correction, preserving dense visual geometry without retraining the foundation model.
AI.result: On nuScenes with 4-frame surround-view input, DrivingDepth achieves AbsRel of 11.19 and EdgeCR of 5.741, outperforming MapAnything (11.99/1.914) in both metric accuracy and geometric consistency.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models already capture coherent relative depth; only per-pixel scale mapping is needed, not full regeneration. DrivingDepth proves this approach simultaneously delivers SOTA metric accuracy and geometric consistency.

title: HVPNet: A Bio-Inspired Network for General Salient and Camouflaged Object Detection
summary: In recent years, most research on multimodal salient object detection (SOD) and camouflaged object detection (COD) typically aims to improve performance through complex cross-modal feature fusion and decoding structures. However, this approach leads to an excessively large model parameter scale and often fails to deliver satisfactory detection performance due to structural redundancy. In contrast, the human visual process is able to efficiently perform salient and camouflaged object identification without such complex structures. This contrast raises an important question: Can we draw conceptual inspiration from the human visual process to achieve a simpler modeling strategy, and still realize accurate and efficient object detection? To answer this question, we propose HVPNet, a simple yet general bio-inspired computational architecture. Drawing on the multi-layered information integration of the retina as a conceptual metaphor, we designed a Retinal Integration Module (RIM), which effectively integrates multimodal features through a level-specific multi-stage integration strategy. To fully exploit these features, we further design a cortical decoder (CD) that breaks down the decoding process into low- and high-level visual stages, abstracting the hierarchical processing in the human visual cortex. Benefiting from these designs, HVPNet can readily extend to seven tasks across four modalities. Without bells and whistles, it establishes an excellent accuracy-efficiency trade-off across 22 datasets spanning these seven tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/jiaweiXu1029/HVPNet.

AI.tldr: HVPNet is a bio-inspired architecture for multimodal salient and camouflaged object detection that simplifies complex cross-modal fusion by drawing from human visual processing. It uses a Retinal Integration Module (RIM) and a Cortical Decoder (CD) to achieve strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across 7 tasks, 4 modalities, and 22 datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal SOD and COD methods rely on complex cross-modal fusion and decoding, leading to large model parameters and structural redundancy, yet often fail to deliver satisfactory performance. The authors question whether simpler, bio-inspired modeling can achieve both accurate and efficient detection.
AI.method: Proposes HVPNet with two key components: (1) Retinal Integration Module (RIM) — a level-specific multi-stage integration strategy conceptually inspired by the retina's multi-layered information integration; (2) Cortical Decoder (CD) — breaks decoding into low- and high-level visual stages, abstracting hierarchical processing in the human visual cortex.
AI.result: Extends to 7 tasks across 4 modalities, achieves an excellent accuracy-efficiency trade-off on 22 datasets spanning these tasks. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Bio-inspired simple architectures can rival or outperform complex cross-modal fusion designs, demonstrating that drawing conceptual inspiration from the human visual process is a promising direction for efficient and accurate object detection.

title: Fully Automated High-Precision Segmentation of Retinal Atrophy and Ellipsoid Zone Thickness in OCT: A Reliable Tool for Real-World GA Monitoring
comment: 31 pages, 6 tables, 7 figures, contain 3 supplemental figures and 2 supplemental tables
summary: Geographic atrophy (GA) secondary to age-related macular degeneration (AMD) requires precise monitoring of relevant structural biomarkers to assess disease stage, progression, and treatment response. This paper presents a fully automated, deep learning-based framework for the high-precision, pixel-wise segmentation of key biomarkers in optical coherence tomography (OCT) imaging: retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) loss, ellipsoid zone (EZ) loss, and EZ thinning. The proposed pipeline uses three specialized semantic segmentation models to delineate RPE loss, EZ boundaries (including interruptions), and Bruch's membrane. To ensure robustness and generalizability, the models were developed on a diverse dataset of 298 SD-OCT volumes representing the full phenotypic spectrum of AMD (GA:222, intermediate AMD: 40, neovascular AMD: 17, healthy: 19) and validated on an independent external dataset (n=43). The comprehensive evaluation was further strengthened using additional datasets to assess repeatability, inter-reader reliability, the impact of B-scan density on measurement accuracy, and subgroup performance stratified by lesion size. Results demonstrated high segmentation accuracy (Dice RPE loss: 0.88, Dice EZ loss: 0.87, Pearson's r > 0.99). Total EZ thickness measurements exhibited a sub-pixel average deviation of 2.15 $μm$, and segmentation reliability was confirmed by a strong reproducibility score (ICC > 0.98). By accurately and consistently quantifying outer photoreceptor degeneration and RPE loss, this fully automated framework provides a highly reliable tool for GA assessment in both clinical trials and routine real-world ophthalmic care.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework automatically segments RPE loss, EZ loss, and EZ thinning in OCT images for GA assessment, achieving high accuracy (Dice >0.87, ICC >0.98) across diverse AMD phenotypes.
AI.motivation: Geographic atrophy (GA) in AMD requires precise monitoring of structural biomarkers (RPE loss, EZ loss, EZ thinning) for disease staging and treatment response, but manual assessment is labor-intensive and variable.
AI.method: Three specialized semantic segmentation models were developed on 298 SD-OCT volumes (full AMD spectrum) to delineate RPE loss, EZ boundaries, and Bruch's membrane, validated on an external dataset (n=43) with additional repeatability/reliability analyses.
AI.result: Dice scores: RPE loss 0.88, EZ loss 0.87; Pearson's r > 0.99; sub-pixel EZ thickness deviation of 2.15 μm; ICC > 0.98 for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The fully automated framework provides a highly reliable tool for GA assessment in clinical trials and routine ophthalmic care by accurately quantifying photoreceptor degeneration and RPE loss.

title: SimpleSearch-VL: A Simple Recipe for Multimodal Agentic Deep Search
summary: We present SimpleSearch-VL, an efficient, reliable, and practical framework for multimodal agentic search. Its core idea is to improve the agent's own search-and-verification process rather than scaling data, tools, or auxiliary model components. For efficiency, Factorized Adaptive Rollout (FAR) improves sampling efficiency by forming more informative training groups while using redundant samples to mitigate long-tail latency and expose hard samples. For reliability, SimpleSearch-VL performs evidence-verified reasoning, explicitly using chain-of-thought verification to assess the relevance of retrieved visual and textual cues to the original context. For practicality, SimpleSearch-VL keeps a lightweight tool interface and performs webpage self-summary within the agent, requiring no additional external dependencies. With only 5K supervised tool-interleaved trajectories and 2K RL data, SimpleSearch-VL improves Qwen3-VL agentic baselines by 15.8 and 16.0 average points for the 8B and 30B-A3B variants, respectively. The SimpleSearch-VL-30B-A3B model further achieves performance competitive with agentic Gemini-3-Pro.

AI.tldr: SimpleSearch-VL improves multimodal agentic search through factorized adaptive rollout for efficiency, evidence-verified reasoning for reliability, and lightweight webpage self-summary for practicality, achieving 15.8-16.0 point gains over Qwen3-VL baselines with minimal data.
AI.motivation: To build an efficient, reliable, and practical multimodal agentic search framework without relying on scaling data, tools, or auxiliary model components.
AI.method: Factorized Adaptive Rollout (FAR) for efficient sampling; evidence-verified reasoning via chain-of-thought for reliability; lightweight tool interface with webpage self-summary for practicality.
AI.result: Improves Qwen3-VL baselines by 15.8 (8B) and 16.0 (30B-A3B) average points using only 5K supervised trajectories and 2K RL data; 30B-A3B variant matches Gemini-3-Pro.
AI.conclusion: SimpleSearch-VL demonstrates that improving the agent's search-and-verification process itself is more effective than scaling data, tools, or model components for multimodal agentic search.
