AI.tldr: FLORA is a deep learning framework using octree-based point cloud processing with auxiliary data fusion to predict six forest attributes from heterogeneous LiDAR data across France, achieving robust results (e.g., R²=0.88 for dominant height) from a single cross-season model.
AI.motivation: National LiDAR programs across Europe face heterogeneous acquisition conditions (varying sensors, flight parameters, seasons, scan angles), making wall-to-wall forest attribute prediction challenging since existing models are typically calibrated for local conditions only.
AI.method: FLORA combines an octree-based backbone for processing heterogeneous LiDAR point clouds with ecological and spatiotemporal auxiliary variables via a late-fusion gating mechanism, trained on 32,052 NFI plots from the French LiDAR HD program.
AI.result: A single model trained on both leaf-on and leaf-off data outperforms season-specific models; auxiliary variables help species-specific volume prediction; FLORA achieves rRMSE of 12.3% (R²=0.88) for dominant height and 39% (R²=0.74) for total volume.
AI.conclusion: FLORA provides a robust baseline for large-scale forest attribute estimation from heterogeneous national LiDAR programs, demonstrating cross-season robustness without needing separate models per acquisition condition.

title: SpheRoPE: Zero-Shot Optimization-Free 360 Panorama Generation with Spherical RoPE
summary: We present a zero-shot, training-free and optimization-free framework for generating 360 panoramic images and videos by directly injecting spherical priors into pre-trained diffusion transformers. Existing methods either rely on costly fine-tuning on scarce panoramic data that limits generalization, or leverage multi-step optimization that incurs prohibitive inference latency. We observe that contemporary generative models natively exhibit some panoramic priors from large-scale training. However, these emergent capabilities are insufficient, as the models fundamentally fail to satisfy the rigorous topological constraints imposed by equirectangular projection (ERP). We introduce a zero-shot and optimization-free approach that resolves these constraints at inference time. Spherical RoPE replaces standard rotary position embeddings: low-frequency channels are re-parameterized as 3D Cartesian coordinates to natively encode the spherical manifold, while high-frequency channels are harmonically quantized to enforce exact periodicity. Coupled with complementary Semantic Distortion classifier-free guidance (CFG) that explicitly steers geometry, we avoid retraining and inherit the full creative breadth of state-of-the-art models. Our approach generalizes across diverse backbones and 360 generation modalities. We demonstrate this across text-to-panorama using Flux.1, Flux.2, and LTX-Video backbones, achieving competitive performance against baselines, all while remaining training-free. Project page: https://orhir.github.io/SpheRoPE

AI.tldr: A zero-shot, training-free framework for generating 360° panoramic images/videos by injecting spherical priors into pre-trained diffusion transformers via Spherical RoPE and Semantic Distortion CFG.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for 360° panoramic generation either require costly fine-tuning on scarce panoramic data (limiting generalization) or use multi-step optimization (high inference latency). The authors aim to achieve high-quality panoramic generation without retraining or optimization.
AI.method: Spherical RoPE replaces standard rotary position embeddings: low-frequency channels re-parameterized as 3D Cartesian coordinates to encode spherical manifold, high-frequency channels harmonically quantized for exact periodicity. Coupled with Semantic Distortion classifier-free guidance (CFG) to steer geometry.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance against baselines across text-to-panorama tasks using Flux.1, Flux.2, and LTX-Video backbones, while remaining training-free and optimization-free.
AI.conclusion: The proposed zero-shot approach generalizes across diverse backbones and 360° generation modalities, inheriting full creative breadth of state-of-the-art models without retraining.

title: PointSplat: Compact Gaussian Splatting via Human-Centric Prediction
summary: Producing 3D human representations from input views on the fly is essential for immersive live streaming systems, where representation compactness is as critical as high fidelity given limited computational power and transmission bandwidth. Although recent feed-forward reconstruction methods achieve impressive quality through the view-centric prediction of 3D representations, they repeatedly encode the same subject content across multiple views, leading to significant inter-view redundancy. Our key insight is to perform predictions directly in 3D space, enabling the network to learn and produce a highly compact representation. To this end, we propose PointSplat, a novel human-centric approach that directly infers Gaussian primitives from an input point set. The proposed method first estimates a coarse geometric proxy and performs ray casting to prune redundant points and establish explicit 2D--3D correspondences. Subsequently, it employs a Point-Image Transformer to fuse appearance and geometry features, predicting Gaussian attributes in a single forward pass. This design restricts predictions to foreground regions of interest, substantially reducing the total number of Gaussians while improving novel-view rendering quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PointSplat achieves higher efficiency and quality while exhibiting strong robustness to variations in view count and image resolution across multiple datasets.

AI.tldr: PointSplat directly predicts compact 3D Gaussian primitives from input point sets via a coarse geometry proxy and Point-Image Transformer, achieving high-quality novel-view synthesis with significantly fewer Gaussians and robust performance across varied view counts and resolutions.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward reconstruction methods encode the same subject repeatedly across multiple views, causing high inter-view redundancy and inefficiency for live streaming where compactness and bandwidth are critical.
AI.method: PointSplat first estimates a coarse geometric proxy and performs ray casting to prune redundant points and establish 2D-3D correspondences. Then, a Point-Image Transformer fuses appearance and geometry features to predict Gaussian attributes in a single forward pass, restricting predictions to foreground regions only.
AI.result: PointSplat achieves higher efficiency and quality than existing methods, while showing strong robustness to varying view counts and image resolutions across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: Direct 3D-space prediction of Gaussian primitives from point sets is an effective strategy for compact and high-fidelity human representations, suitable for immersive live streaming systems.

title: GEAR: Guided End-to-End AutoRegression for Image Synthesis
summary: Visual generative models are typically trained in two stages. A tokenizer is first trained for reconstruction and then frozen, after which a generator is trained on its discrete indices or continuous latents. This decoupling leaves the tokenizer unaware of what the generator finds easy to model. We present GEAR (Guided End-to-end AutoRegression), which trains a vector-quantized (VQ) tokenizer and an autoregressive (AR) generator jointly and end-to-end, guided by representation alignment. The key obstacle is that the VQ index fed to the AR model is non-differentiable, so gradients cannot reach the tokenizer, and a straight-through estimator collapses. GEAR resolves this with a dual read-out of the codebook assignment. A hard, one-hot branch trains the AR with next-token prediction, while a differentiable soft branch carries a representation-alignment loss that flows back to guide only the tokenizer. The AR model thereby steers its tokenizer toward an index distribution it can predict more easily. This shifts the alignment burden from the tokenizer to the AR: the tokenizer's own features become less DINOv2-like while the AR's become more so, the opposite of diffusion-side recipes that make the latent itself semantic. GEAR speeds up ImageNet gFID convergence by up to 10x relative to the strong LlamaGen-REPA baseline, learns markedly better patch-level and spatially-coherent features, and generalizes across quantizers (VQVAE, LFQ, IBQ) and to text-to-image generation.

AI.tldr: GEAR jointly trains VQ tokenizer and autoregressive generator end-to-end via representation alignment, using a dual read-out (hard branch for AR training, soft branch for guiding tokenizer), achieving up to 10x faster ImageNet gFID convergence over LlamaGen-REPA.
AI.motivation: Current visual generative models train tokenizer and generator separately in two stages, where the tokenizer is unaware of what the generator finds easy to model, leading to suboptimal alignment between the two components.
AI.method: GEAR uses a dual read-out of the codebook assignment: a hard one-hot branch trains the AR with next-token prediction, while a differentiable soft branch carries a representation-alignment loss to guide only the tokenizer, enabling end-to-end joint training despite the non-differentiable VQ index.
AI.result: GEAR achieves up to 10x faster ImageNet gFID convergence compared to LlamaGen-REPA baseline, learns better patch-level and spatially-coherent features, and generalizes across different quantizers (VQVAE, LFQ, IBQ) and to text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end joint training of tokenizer and generator with representation alignment shifts the alignment burden from tokenizer to AR model, producing more coherent features and significantly faster convergence, demonstrating a promising direction for visual generative modeling.

title: FaceMoE: Mixture of Experts for Low-Resolution Face Recognition
summary: Low-resolution face recognition (LR-FR) remains a challenging task due to poor feature extraction and aggregation, as probe images often contain limited identity information resulting from extreme degradations such as blur, occlusion, and low contrast. Additionally, the domain gap between high-resolution (HR) gallery images and low-resolution (LR) probe images poses a significant challenge. A single feature encoder struggles to generalize effectively across both domains when fine-tuned on an LR dataset, and this issue is further magnified by catastrophic forgetting. To address these challenges, we propose FaceMoE, an effective adaptation of Mixture of Experts (MoE) transfomer architecture for low-resolution face-recognition . Specifically, we introduce multiple specialized feed-forward network (FFN) experts and incorporate a top-k router, which dynamically assigns tokens to appropriate experts. This design emergently promotes specialization across experts for different semantic regions of the face, which enables FaceMoE to perform resolution-aware feature extraction. Moreover, the top-k router facilitates sparse expert activation, enabling the model to preserve pretrained knowledge when finetuned on a LR dataset, while increasing model capacity without proportional computational overhead. FaceMoE is trained with a combined face recognition loss, router z-loss, and load balancing loss to ensure expert specialization and stable training. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work leveraging MoE for LR-FR. Extensive experiments across eleven datasets, spanning HR, mixed-quality, and LR benchmarks, demonstrate that FaceMoE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code: https://github.com/Kartik-3004/FaceMoE

AI.tldr: FaceMoE introduces a Mixture of Experts Transformer architecture for low-resolution face recognition, using specialized FFN experts and a top-k router to dynamically assign tokens, enabling resolution-aware feature extraction and mitigating catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning. It outperforms SOTA across 11 datasets.
AI.motivation: LR-FR is challenging due to poor feature extraction from degraded probe images (blur, occlusion, low contrast) and the domain gap between HR gallery and LR probe images. Single feature encoders struggle to generalize across both domains, and catastrophic forgetting exacerbates this issue.
AI.method: FaceMoE adapts the MoE Transformer architecture by introducing multiple specialized FFN experts and a top-k router that dynamically assigns tokens to appropriate experts. This promotes specialization across semantic face regions for resolution-aware feature extraction. Sparse expert activation preserves pretrained knowledge and increases capacity without proportional compute overhead. Training combines face recognition loss, router z-loss, and load balancing loss.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 11 datasets (HR, mixed-quality, LR benchmarks) show FaceMoE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. This is the first work leveraging MoE for LR-FR.
AI.conclusion: FaceMoE effectively addresses LR-FR challenges through MoE-based dynamic token routing and expert specialization, achieving superior performance while maintaining efficient computation and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
